coredata.dc:title: Spatial/electronic symmetry breaking of metal-support catalysts for efficient water dissociation and alkaline hydrogen spillover

coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen spillover on metal-support surface has been demonstrated to boost the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in proton-rich acidic environments. However, the electron-poor metal state due to electron transfer from metal to support strictly limits proton adsorption/spillover in alkaline conditions. Here, we propose a novel approach to induce alkaline hydrogen spillover based on spatial/electronic symmetry breaking and report their realization in Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C system. The introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) breaks the symmetry of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P system, resulting in a Janus electronic state in Ru nanoclusters: e--enriched region, Rutop and e--deficient region, Ruinterf, which are respectively crucial for local proton enrichment and hydrogen spillover. Resultantly, the HER is efficiently implemented with a low overpotential of 20 mV at 10 mA cm−2 for 45 h and a small Tafel slope of 26.5 mV dec−1 via facilitated alkaline hydrogen spillover. Our work opens the way to induce hydrogen spillover for HER in alkaline environment based on symmetry breaking.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrations of the interfacial electronic configurations and symmetry-breaking mechanisms rationalize the design of metal-support catalysts for alkaline hydrogen spillover. (a, b) Theoretical explanation of the influence of large and small ΔEf on the interfacial electronic configurations. Evac = vacuum energy, Ec = conduction band, Ev = valence band, Ef-M = Fermi level of metal, Ef-S = Fermi level of support. (c) conventional metal-support structure with a symmetric interface, and (d) CQDs modified metal-support structure with an asymmetric interface to facilitate hydrogen spillover.

Structural characterization of the fabricated Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C. (a) Schematic illustration for the synthesis process of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C. (b, c) SEM of NiPOx, and Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C. (d) TEM of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C (Inset: the EDX spectroscopy line-scan profile of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C), (e, f) HAADF-STEM images of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, (g) The corresponding intensity profiles of 1, 2 and 3 in (f), (h) the HAADF-STEM and elemental mapping images of the Ni, Ru, P, and C elements for Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C.

Structure and phase composition for Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C. (a) XRD spectra, (b) FT-IR spectra, and (c) UV–vis of the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C. The inset in (c) shows the photographs of the peeled Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P (left) and Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C (right) solution under UV light at 365 nm. XPS spectra of the (d) C 1 s, (e) Ni 2p, and (f) Ru 3p regions for Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C. (g) Atomic model of Ru-Ni2P/C and Ru-Ni2P. (h, i) Work functions of Ni2P, Ru and C/Ru.

The electrocatalytic measurements of different samples for HER electrocatalysis in 1 M KOH. (a) Polarization curves, (b) comparison of η10 and η100, (c) tafel plots, (d) Nyquist plots, and (e) electrochemical double-layer capacitance of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P, Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, Pt/C and Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C. (f) The polarization curves of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C before and after 2000 cycles CV stability test, inset of (f) showing long-term durability test for Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C at −10 mA cm−2 for 120 h. (g) comparison of η10 in 1 M KOH for Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C with recently-reported Ru-based and Ni2P-based HER electrocatalysts.

Evidence for the existence of hydrogen spillover. (a) LSV curves measured in KOH electrolytes with pH = 12, 12.5, 13, and 13.5 (left), Plots of log j at −0.2 V (vs. RHE) vs. pH for Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P, and Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C catalysts (right), from which the proton reaction orders (ρRHE = ∂logj/∂pH) were derived, (b) CV of the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P, Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, and Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C catalysts in 1 M KOH with a scan rate of 50 mV/s, (c) Bode plots for the group of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P, Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, Pt/C and Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C catalysts, (d) electrochemical double-layer capacitance Plots of Cφ vs η of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P, Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, and Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C in 1 M KOH. (e) EIS-derived Tafel plots of the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P, Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, and Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C catalysts obtained from the hydrogen adsorption resistance R2. (f) In situ Raman spectra of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C recorded in 1 M KOH from −0.9 to −1.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl. (g) illustration of hydrogen spillover.

Experimental and theoretical insights for water electrolysis mechanisms. (a) Electric field vector and electric field strength maps of the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C catalyst constructed from the DPC-STEM images. The inset color wheel represents vector direction and magnitude by its color and brightness, respectively, (b) Electric field intensity profile along the white line shown in (a), (c) The charge density difference and Bader charge analysis of Ru-Ni2P/C and Ru-Ni2P, (d) The Gibbs free-energy diagrams of water dissociation in Ru-Ni2P/C and Ru-Ni2P, (e) Schematic illustration of reaction mechanism via atomic-scale hydrogen spillover on Ru-Ni2P/C. (f) Calculated free energy diagram for hydrogen spillover on Ru-Ni2P/C.

head.ce:title: Spatial/electronic symmetry breaking of metal-support catalysts for efficient water dissociation and alkaline hydrogen spillover

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen spillover on metal-support surface has been demonstrated to boost the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in proton-rich acidic environments. However, the electron-poor metal state due to electron transfer from metal to support strictly limits proton adsorption/spillover in alkaline conditions. Here, we propose a novel approach to induce alkaline hydrogen spillover based on spatial/electronic symmetry breaking and report their realization in Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C system. The introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) breaks the symmetry of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P system, resulting in a Janus electronic state in Ru nanoclusters: e--enriched region, Rutop and e--deficient region, Ruinterf, which are respectively crucial for local proton enrichment and hydrogen spillover. Resultantly, the HER is efficiently implemented with a low overpotential of 20 mV at 10 mA cm−2 for 45 h and a small Tafel slope of 26.5 mV dec−1 via facilitated alkaline hydrogen spillover. Our work opens the way to induce hydrogen spillover for HER in alkaline environment based on symmetry breaking.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A novel spatial/electronic symmetry-breaking strategy is developed to realize a Janus electronic state in Ru nanoclusters for efficient alkaline hydrogen spillover. As a result, the CQDs-induced asymmetry interfaces in the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C system achieve a low overpotential of 20 mV at 10 mA cm−2 for 45 h and a small Tafel slope of 26.5 mV dec−1, facilitated by efficient alkaline hydrogen spillover.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) is an ideal candidate to replace traditional fossil fuels owing to its high gravimetric energy density and carbon-free characteristic in pursuit of carbon neutrality [1,2]. One of the eco-friendly and sustainable strategies to produce hydrogen is water electrolysis driven by renewable electricity, which includes hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), one half-reaction of water-splitting [3,4]. Compared to acidic HER, alkaline/neutral conditions show greater potential for industrial applications, due to their lower corrosiveness and the ability to utilize a wider range of catalyst materials, including non-noble metals [5]. Among these, Alkaline Water Electrolysis (AWE) is particularly regarded as more sustainable and environmentally friendly for large-scale deployment [6]. However, the alkaline proton-deficient microenvironment severely restricts the proton-generation-evolution during HER, leading to slower kinetics compared to acidic conditions [7–9]. For instance, even platinum (Pt), the state-of-the-art HER catalyst, shows about 3 orders of magnitude lower activity in alkaline condition as compared to acidic one [10–12].

HER in alkaline environment starts from the dissociation of water molecules to supply protons, followed by H2 formation via either the Heyrovsky reaction or the Tafel reaction [13,14]. Accelerating the dissociation of water molecules is thus a prerequisite to improve HER kinetics, which is also the focus of previous research [15–17], and various methods have been proposed, such as designing unique topological structure to expose more active sites [18], constructing heterointerface [19,20], and incorporating a specific component onto the catalytically active species [21]. In practical applications, catalysts generally operate at large current densities (> 500 mA cm−2) under large overpotentials [22,23]. In this case, catalytically active sites will be covered by a large number of adsorbed hydrogen atoms from water-splitting, which impedes the subsequent adsorption of water molecules. Fortunately, this can be alleviated by hydrogen spillover, a migration process of activated hydrogen atoms from a hydrogen-rich metal to a hydrogen-poor support surface [24,25].

Recently, hydrogen spillover strategy has been taken into account for the advanced catalysts design in acidic environment owing to abundant hydrogen ions, hydrogen spillover is easy to occur, i.e., hydrogen atoms migrate from the metal surface to its support surface, as exemplified in systems like Co-TiOx/Ti[26], Pt/TiO2 [27] and PtIr/CoP[28]. However, the situation is quite different. Generally, the metal catalyst (especially the top part) supported on semiconductor is in an electron(e-)-deficient state due to a typical electron transfer (M → S) caused by their Fermi energy (Ef) difference ( Fig. 1a) [29,30]. This seriously hampers the adsorption of protons, created by dissociation of H2O molecules, thus resulting into a proton-deficient state of the metal surface (Fig. 1c) [31]. Furthermore, the large ΔEf will lead to interfacial charge accumulation which causes the strong reactive hydrogen species (H*) trapping at interface, leading to a large kinetic barrier for the interfacial hydrogen spillover (Fig. 1a, b). These are the fundamental reasons that hydrogen spillover is difficult to occur in systems consisting of metal catalysts supported on semiconductors, and thus have rarely been reported in alkaline HER [32–34].

Here, we demonstrate that breaking the symmetry is an effective strategy to promote efficient hydrogen spillover in alkaline electrolyte, by taking Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C as a model. The introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) breaks the symmetry of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P system from two aspects: 1) geometric structural symmetry, 2) electronic state symmetry, i.e., forming a Janus electronic state in Ru nanoclusters (e--enriched region, Rutop and e--deficient region, Ruinterf), which is crucial for proton enrichment and hydrogen spillover, as shown in Fig. 1d. Experimental evidences and DFT calculations definitively verify that the symmetry-breaking caused by CQDs induces an efficient hydrogen spillover in alkaline environment, owing to the creating local acidic environment around the top of Ru nanoparticles and e--deficient interface region. As a result, the well-designed 3D, hierarchical Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C nanotube-array delivers a current density of 10 mA cm−2 at an ultralow overpotential of 20 mV, and a small Tafel slope of 24.4 mV dec−1 in 1.0 M KOH solution, surpassing the state-of-the-art traditional metal-supported catalysts and commercial Pt catalyst ever reported. This research pioneers a novel approach through breaking symmetry to realize efficient hydrogen spillover in alkaline electrolyte for industrial hydrogen production.

Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C was prepared by a three-step method as schematically illustrated in Fig. 2a. Typically, free-standing NiPOx/NF nanorod arrays were first synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method [35]. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Fig. 2b and Fig. S1) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. S2a) of the NiPOx/NF exhibit solid nanorod arrays with smooth surfaces vertically grown on nickel foam (NF). The selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern shows regular bright spots (Fig. S2b) corresponding to the (-222), (002), and (-220) facets of Ni(PO3)2, indicating the well-defined single-crystal character. Then, a carefully designed self-assembly process was implemented to delicately fabricate a three-dimensional hierarchical nanotube-array through a subsequent hydrothermal treatment, employing NiPOx/NF as a template. During this process, the NiPOx nanorod was slowly etched by KOH from the exterior to the interior, followed by an Ru3+ ion exchange process to fabricate the 3D hierarchical hollow nanotube-array, while the citric acid and NaOH were slowly pyrolyzed and carbonized to produce partially graphitized carbon dots. Finally, followed by a heat treatment in PH3/Ar mixed atmosphere, the 3D hierarchical super structure integrated ultra-thin 2D nanosheets and 1D hollow nanotube-array morphology of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C was eventually manufactured (Fig. S3-S5).

We first conducted the SEM (Fig. 2c) and TEM (Fig. 2d, e) measurements to probe the morphological information of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, which displays hollow nanotube-array with internal void diameters of approximately 150 nm integrated with loose nanosheets. This specific hierarchical hollow nanotube-array structure may facilitate electrolyte penetration and bubble release, as well as increasing the number of active site exposures [36–38]. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. S6a) reveals abundant grain boundaries within the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C composite, consisting of numerous interconnected ultra-small nanoparticles approximately 2.0 nm in size. Furthermore, distinct multi-phase heterojunction interfaces are evident in the interfacial region (Fig. S6b-f). The aberration-corrected high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) image (Fig. 2e) demonstrates that dense hetero-interfaces are widely distributed across the surface nanosheets. Bright speckles with a diameter of ∼2.0 nm, corresponding to Ru clusters, further confirm the successful synthesis of the metal-support catalyst involving Ru clusters. The aberration-corrected HAADF-STEM analysis (Fig. 2f) clearly displays the Ru cluster-support nanostructure with varying Z-contrast, highlighting the different elemental compositions. To determine the precise composition of the metal-support (M-S) nanostructure, detailed analyses were conducted on various regions. As shown in Fig. 2g, bright speckles with interplanar distances (d-spacings) of 0.233 nm can be indexed to the (100) plane of the Ru phase, while the outer support regions with d-spacings of 0.221 nm and 0.214 nm correspond to the (111) plane of Ni2P and the (100) plane of carbon, respectively. Interestingly, the introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) effectively disrupted the structural symmetry of the Ru-Ni2P metal-supported catalyst system. The selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. S6c) of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C exhibits clear diffraction rings corresponding to the (111) plane of Ni2P, the (100) plane of Ru, and the (102) plane of graphitized carbon, along with distinct spots representing Bragg reflections from Ni5P4. Additionally, the low-magnification HAADF-STEM image and corresponding elemental mapping results (Fig. 2h) show that Ni, Ru, P, and C elements are predominantly located at the exterior of the nanorod, consistent with the line-scanning spectra (inset in Fig. 2d), further confirming the specific hollow structure.

The crystal structure of the as-prepared Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C catalyst was examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 3a, the characteristic peaks of the as-synthesized Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C can be respectively indexed to Ni5P4 (PDF#18–0883), Ni2P (PDF#03–0953), and Ru (PDF#06–0663), which is in accordance with the HRTEM results. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 3b) of the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C shows stretching vibrations of C-H2 (2962 and 2839 cm−1), C-C (1458 cm−1), and C-O-C (1381 cm−1) functional groups, indicating the formation of graphitized carbon [39]. In the UV–visible absorption spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. 3c), an absorption maximum/band is observed at 275 nm, which is attributed to the π→π* transition of the aromatic C-C bond [40]. When excited by 360 nm light, the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C composite demonstrates blue luminescence [41]. The results of FTIR spectra and UV–visible absorption spectroscopy co-verify the successful introduction of the graphitized carbon dots. Thus, all above-mentioned results demonstrate the successful formation of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C hierarchical hollow nanotube heterostructure arrays.

To further investigate the chemical composition and electronic structure of the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted. The XPS survey spectrum of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C (Fig. S8a) confirms the coexistence of Ni, Ru, P, O, and C elements. The XPS spectra of C 1 s (Fig. 3d) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at approximately 284.8, 286.55, and 288.67 eV, corresponding to C-C, C-P, and O-C-O, respectively [42]. As for the Ni 2p spectra (Fig. 3e), the peaks can be fitted with two pairs spin-orbit doublets of Ni2+ and Ni3+ peaks with binding energies approximately at 874.73 eV/856.84 eV and 878.31 eV/859.94 eV, respectively. Additionally, the peak at around 853.36 eV can be assigned to Ni2P [43,44]. Interestingly, the binding energy of Ni 2p spectra shifts to a higher position after the introduction of CQDs, which is ascribed to the stronger electronegativity of C atoms compared with Ni element, resulting in the transfer of local electrons around Ni to C. Similarly, the P 2p peaks (Fig. S8b) assigned to the phosphorus anion and the Ni-P bond, shifts to a higher level after the introduction of CQDs species due to stronger electronegativity. The Ru 3p (Fig. 3 f) peaks with binding energies around 461.0 and 483.8 eV can readily be assigned to the Ru 3p3/2 and Ru 3p1/2 signals of metallic Ru [45], which shifts to a lower position after the introduction of CQDs. XPS results show that the introduction of CQDs and the construction of heterostructure, resulting in a stronger electronic interaction, is an effective strategy for regulating the electronic structure of the catalyst [46].

When metal and support are in contact and form a tight interface, the free electrons spontaneously diffuse from the one with higher Ef into the other with lower Ef until a new equilibrium is formed, ultimately resulting in the formation of an internal electrostatic field [25]. Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations (Fig. 3 g) was conducted to predict the work function (Wf) values of Ru-Ni2P and Ru-Ni2P/C. The calculated work function difference (∆Φ) between Ru nanoparticle and Ni2P support is sharply reduced to 0.14 eV due to the introduction of C (Fig. 3 h, i), suggesting that C promotes spontaneous charge transfer from Ni2P to Ru. Such a small ∆Φ barrier will weaken the charge accumulation and proton capture at the interface to rationalize the hydrogen spillover.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HER performance of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C in alkaline media
ce:section.ce:para: The HER performance of the as-prepared electrocatalysts was examined in a three-electrode system in 1.0 M KOH [47]. In comparison to Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P and commercial Pt/C (5.0 wt%), the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C exhibits a greatly improved HER activity ( Fig. 4a). What is more, it only acquires overpotentials of 20 and 120 mV at current densities of 10 and 100 mA cm−2, respectively, which is much lower than the Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P and commercial Pt/C as displayed in Fig. 4b. Meanwhile, Tafel plots are performed to examine the kinetics and reaction mechanism. As shown in Fig. 4c, the Tafel slope for Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C (26.5 mV dec−1) is smaller than that for commercial Pt/C (31.5 mV dec−1), implying faster HER rates. As generally accepted that the theoretical Tafel slope is 30 mV dec−1, 40 mV dec−1 and 120 mV dec−1 for Tafel, Heyrovsky and Volmer step, respectively. Such a small Tafel slop value of 26.5 mV dec−1 suggests that the alkaline HER electrocatalysis of the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C catalyst may follow a reaction mechanism of Volmer-Tafel. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) performed in Fig. 4d was conducted to contrast the radii of the curves to investigate the charge transfer abilities of the electrocatalysts. The smallest radius of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C reveals a higher electrical conductivity and faster electron transfer kinetics which is a key factor for the boosting HER performance. To explore the electrochemical active area of different catalysts, the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) was measured by CV method. As demonstrated in Fig. 4e, the Cdl values of Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P and Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C are determined to be 5.2, 6.5, and 10.5 mF cm−2, respectively (calculated based on corresponding CV curves in Fig. S9). The much higher Cdl indicates the more exposed catalytically active sites and the more efficient adsorption and transferring reactants in the surface of the electrocatalysts. Apart from the superior HER activity, catalytic stability is another important criterion for practical application. The stability test was performed by applying chronoamperometric and CV scanning tests in 1.0 M KOH. The polarization curve of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C is well retained after 2000 CV scanning cycles (Fig. 4f, inset). Besides, the chronoamperometric curves in Fig. 4f indicate that after 120 h of testing at the overpotential of 20 mV, a negligible decayed current density is observed, revealing the outstanding electrochemical stability of the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C electrode. In addition, the SEM image (Fig. S11) of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C after chronoamperometry test retains the pristine hollow nanorod morphology with a rougher surface, which is consistent with the TEM images (Fig. S12a, b). According to the analysis of HRTEM image in Fig. S9c, the fringe spacings of 0.225, 0.214, 0.229 and 0.332 nm match the lattice plane of Ni2P, Ru, Ni5P4 and C species, suggesting the well-retained heterostructure after long-term HER test. Furthermore, the XRD (Fig. S13) and XPS (Fig. S14) spectrums present invisible change which also suggesting the robust stabilities during the HER process. When compared to the reported noble-metal based and Ni2P-based HER catalysts (Table S2), the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C catalyst also exhibits superior activity for HER in alkaline conditions (Fig. 4g). The above results confirm that the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C can be used as HER electrocatalysts with high efficiency and outstanding stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental understanding of improved HER kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: To dig out the origin of the enhanced HER activity of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C in alkaline electrolyte, a series of comprehensive experiments and electrochemical measurements were performed. The Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C catalyst manifests a remarkably low Tafel slope of merely 26.5 mV dec−1, significantly undercutting the conventional threshold of 30 mV dec−1 associated with the standard Volmer-Heyrovsky/Tafel mechanism [34,48–50]. This deviation intimates the probable engagement of a hydrogen spillover mechanism, a phenomenon echoed in prior research. This speculative reaction mechanism gains additional substantiation from the observed pH dependence of the HER. Specifically, the linear relationship depicted in Fig. 5a, illustrating log | j @ - 200 mV vs. RHE | vs. pH, points to a reaction order of 1.2 for Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P and 1.6 for Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, thereby suggesting a progression towards the theoretical order of 2. This further corroborates the hypothesis involving a hydrogen spillover effect [26,32,51].

To furnish further corroboration of the hydrogen spillover effect, we conducted operando cyclic voltammetry (CV) studies in the double-layer region to scrutinize the hydrogen adsorption and desorption dynamics on the catalysts [28,52]. As revealed in the CV profiles (Fig. 5b and Fig. S15), a conspicuous distinction emerges when comparing Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P and Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C with Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, manifesting in the presence of distinct proton insertion and extraction peaks [48]. This notable observation underscores the pivotal roles of constructing heterointerfaces and introducing CQDs in facilitating proton insertion/extraction from the Ni5P4-Ni2P matrix. A meticulous comparison of the onset potentials for these processes reveals an intriguing pattern: the integration of CQDs onto Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P results in a less positive onset potential for proton insertion while a more negative onset potential for proton extraction (Fig. 5b). This dual-effect of CQDs highlights their capacity to not only expedite proton insertion but also to accelerate proton extraction, thereby underscoring their catalytic prowess. The CV curves for Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C further reveal a scan rate-dependent shift in the proton insertion/extraction peaks (Fig. S15a), indicative of dynamic kinetic control. To quantify these kinetics, we employed the equation i = aν b , where i is the current response, a is an adjustment coefficient, ν is the sweep rate and the power exponent b is a parameter to analyze the kinetics [53,54]. Plotting log(i) against log(ν) yields b values of 1.95 and 1.8 (Fig. S15b) corresponding to the oxidation and reduction peaks, respectively. These values suggest a prevailing pseudocapacitive nature of the proton extraction/insertion processes, with proton extraction occurring at a more rapid rate than insertion in the case of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C [49]. Therefore, the expedited kinetics of hydrogen desorption exhibited by Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C can be ascribed to the proficient hydrogen spillover mechanism, which, in conjunction with the tailored heterointerface and strategic use of CQDs, fosters a highly dynamic environment conducive to rapid proton management.

Operando electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to delve deeper into the hydrogen adsorption kinetics across the fabricated catalysts at varying overpotentials. Fig. 5c illustrates how the Bode plots chart the phase angle's fluctuation with frequency changes, demarcated into two zones: the high-frequency (yellow) and low-frequency (cyan) domains [55]. Prior studies have designated the high-frequency signals as indicative of the catalyst's internal layer or interface electronic conductivity, while the low-frequency counterparts relate to charge transfer resistance at the electrolyte-catalyst interface. It is obvious that the high phase angel in high frequency area and very low phase angel in low frequency area for Pt/C catalysts indicate that the electron transfer resistance of HER is mainly contributed from catalyst inner-layer electron transfer resistance rather than the electrolyte-catalyst interface charge transfer resistance. As for Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, the lower phase angel in high frequency area indicates that Ni5P4-Ni2P/C possesses a faster electronic transfer and the interface charge transfer dominates the reaction resistance of HER. Upon incorporating Ru nanoparticles, Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P witnesses a distinct reduction in phase angle within the low-frequency area, and Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C further enhances both electronic transfer and interface charge transfer, implicating the CQDs' role in accelerating hydrogen adsorption/desorption kinetics.

Following a previous recognition, the obtained EIS data was simulated using a double-parallel equivalent circuit model as shown in Fig. S16-18 and Table S1. The first parallel components (T and R1) reflect the charge-transfer kinetics, in which T is related to the double layer capacitance and R1 represents the catalytic charge-transfer resistance [56]. Low R1 values across all catalysts affirm Ni5P4-Ni2P matrix's conductivity, fostering rapid charge transfer for HER. The second parallel components (Cφ and R2) reflect the hydrogen adsorption behavior on catalyst surface, with Cφ and R2 denoting pseudo-capacitance and resistance for hydrogen adsorption, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5d, the integration of Cφ vs. η profiles provides information on the hydrogen adsorption charge (QH) on the catalyst surface during HER [57]. The marginal increase in adsorbed hydrogen on Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P (QH[Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P] = 99 mC) relative to Ni5P4-Ni2P/C (QH[Ni5P4-Ni2P/C] = 51 mC) suggests limited hydrogen spillover from Ru to Ni5P4-Ni2P. Strikingly, Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C exhibits a substantial rise in adsorbed hydrogen (QH[Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C] = 633 mC) compared to Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P, affirming CQDs' profound impact on promoting hydrogen spillover. Furthermore, analyzing the potential-dependent R2 for all catalysts and plotting log R2 vs. overpotential (Fig. 5e), we observe a reduced slope for Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, indicative of accelerated hydrogen adsorption kinetics [28]. This acceleration aligns with the facilitated hydrogen spillover effect, thus underscoring the pivotal role of CQDs in enhancing HER performance through optimized hydrogen management.

In-situ Raman spectroscopy was employed to scrutinize the surface chemical bonding alterations of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C within a potential window from −0.9 to −1.1 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) during chronoamperometric testing (Fig. 5f). Initially, the Raman signature of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C reveals a broad D band at 1326 cm−1 and a G band at 1584 cm−1 (Fig. S19), indicative of defective carbon structures and sp2 hybridized carbons characteristic of CQDs [42]. As the applied potential gradually decreases, increments in the intensities of the 432 and 520 cm−1 peaks, attributed to Ru-O vibrations, suggest a heightened formation of surface Ru-O bonds [58]. Notably, the intensity of the peak at 1650 cm−1, assigned to the bending vibration of H-OH in adsorbed water, grows in proportion to the applied potential [59]. These observations further confirm the strong OH-/H2O adsorption on Ru sites of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C. Furthermore, new Raman peaks emerge at ∼714 and 812 cm−1 in Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, corresponding to the interactions between adsorbed OH species (*OH) with Ru surfaces [60]. The acquisition of adsorbed *OH on Ru surfaces during alkaline HER provides direct spectral evidence for the efficient adsorption and dissociation of water by the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C catalyst, indicating the presence of local acidic environments that facilitate alkaline hydrogen spillover [61]. Collectively, the pronounced adsorption of OH-/H2O molecules by Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C, fosters a proton-rich microenvironment around the catalyst, expediting the hydrogen spillover process and thereby enhancing HER kinetics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental and theoretical insights of hydrogen spillover in HER
ce:section.ce:para: As proposed in Fig. 1, the Janus electronic state of the support Ru could synergize the alkaline hydrogen spillover processes. To gain the experimental evidence, we measured local electric fields on the surface of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C catalysts using differential phase contrast-scanning transmission electron microscopy (DPC-STEM). The color map in Fig. 6a shows the non-uniform distribution of the electric field (EF) around a single Ru NP on Ni2P/C [62]. The linear profile in Fig. 6b shows the intensity of the electric field across a single Ru particle. Notably, the EF near the edge of the substrate appears weaker, whereas inside the Ru NP, there is a pronounced enhancement of the EF [63]. This observation intuitively demonstrates bidirectional electron transfer and confirms the Janus-like electronic state within the Ru NPs, characterized by distinct regions of electron enrichment and depletion. To gain atomic-scale insight into the origin of the exceptional intrinsic activity of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C for HER in alkaline, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out. Based on prior structural data and the analysis of HRTEM and SAED, the optimized bare metal-support slab model and CQDs modified model structures are shown in Fig. S20-21. To fully investigate the electronic structure-intrinsic activity underpinnings of the interfacial effects, the details regarding the Bader charge and the differential charge density were first analyzed. It is found that the high-density electron-cloud is concentrated at the interface for Ru-Ni2P system (Fig. 6b). Generally, active hydrogen species with the unsaturated electrons on 1 s orbital is easily trapped at the electron-rich region. Therefore, the electron accumulation at the interface of Ru and Ni2P, due to the large difference in their work functions (∆Φ) (Fig. 3 h), brings a strong bond between proton and interfacial sites, thus limiting the interfacial hydrogen spillover. Comparatively, the introduction of CQDs to construct the asymmetric interface triggers a significant redistribution of electrons, which is characterized by the vanish of the interfacial concentrated electron-cloud and directionally migrated electron-cloud towards Rutop and Ni2P substrate. The diluted electron-cloud at the interface of Ru-Ni2P/C is highly consistent with the view that the small ∆Φ (Fig. 3i) can restrain the interfacial charge-transfer and minimize the interfacial electron accumulation [64]. The Bader charge analysis (Fig. 6c) further confirm that the Ru atoms at the interface (Ruinterf) are revealed to donate electrons to both the Ni2P support and top-region Ru atoms (Rutop) after the decoration of C. This bidirectional electron transfer manner induces the formation of e--deficient Ruinterf region and e--rich Rutop region, verifying the hypothesis of Janus electronic state. Therefore, the introduction of CQDs effectively fabricates an asymmetric interface that modulates interfacial electron interaction, consolidating the Janus electronic state. This unique electronic configuration is instrumental in promoting proton enrichment at the top and concurrently facilitating proton dilution at the interface. Specifically, the electron-enriched Rutop region fosters a localized, acidic microenvironment abundant in protons, conducive to alkaline hydrogen spillover, while the electron-deficient Ruinterf region will mitigate the resistance of proton migration.

Given that H2O adsorption is essential for alkaline HER, we calculated the water adsorption energy (ΔEH2O) (Fig. S22). Notably, the more negative ΔEH2O of Ru-Ni2P/C (-0.69 eV) compared to Ru-Ni2P (-0.60 eV) signifies superior water adsorption capabilities. Furthermore, the projected density of states (PDOS) for Ru-Ni2P/C (Fig. S23) exhibits a higher d-band center at −1.43 eV, closer to the Fermi level than Ru-Ni2P (-1.45 eV), reinforcing its enhanced water adsorption and favoring water dissociation; and meanwhile, Ru-Ni2P/C also present the most energetic water dissociation kinetics (0.75 eV, Fig. 6d). The more favorable water dissociation kinetic and regulated Janus electron state for Ru-Ni2P/C benefits the hydrogen spillover mechanism based on multiple sites. Specific reaction site analyses were further conducted to understand the hydrogen spillover in HER of Ru-Ni2P/C (Fig. 6f). At site A, ΔGH* = -0.20, Rutop as a proton activator shows the strong H* adsorption behavior; at site B, ΔGH* increases gradually to −0.11, and its value at the Ni2P substrate is sufficient to be > 0, resulting in the H* desorption tendency on the substrate [65]. Based on these DFT results, a possible HER catalytic mechanism for Ru-Ni2P/C in alkaline media is proposed, leveraging the synergy of multi-functional catalytic sites, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 6e. Specifically, the asymmetry of Ru nanoparticles modified by CQDs exhibits superior electrocatalytic activity during the Volmer process, facilitating effective H2O dissociation. Through a spillover mechanism, the Janus electron state at the Rutop site promotes proton enrichment, while the electron-deficient Ruinterf region favors hydrogen spillover from the Ru site to the Ni2P substrate. Here, the exceptional electrocatalytic activity of Ni2P during the Tafel process is advantageously harnessed to efficiently combine the transferred H* into H2.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized a 3D hollow, hierarchical multi-boundary super Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C complex with exceptional HER performance in alkaline medium, featuring an ultralow overpotential of 20 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a small Tafel slope of 26.5 mV dec−1 in 1.0 M KOH solution, surpassing the state-of-the-art traditional metal-supported catalysts ever reported. By coupling DFT calculations and comprehensive experiments, it is found that the asymmetric MS interface modified with CQDs effectively establishes the Janus electronic state of Ru, thereby facilitating a proficient hydrogen spillover effect in the Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C composite under alkaline conditions. Consequently, the heightened HER catalytic activity of Ru-Ni5P4-Ni2P/C in alkaline is potentially attributed to an unusual atomic-scale hydrogen spillover effect between multiple catalytic sites, where the electron-enriched Rutop region effectively enriches protons to form a localized acidic microenvironment, while the electron-deficient Ruinterf region alleviates interface charge accumulation to facilitate efficient migration of reactive hydrogen atoms onto the Ni-based support, ultimately favoring the release of H2. Our research enhances understanding of how asymmetric interface engineering optimizes metal-support catalysts to facilitate hydrogen spillover and paves the way for innovative strategies in designing highly efficient catalysts.
